verdict_reasoning: B2M (beta-2-microglobulin) is the non-covalent light chain of MHC class I complexes, which are stably expressed on the surface of nearly all nucleated cells. The NCBI summary explicitly states it is 'found in association with the MHC class I heavy chain on the surface of nearly all nucleated cells.' Multiple literature sources confirm surface display: PMID 34971932 notes 'B2M binding to HLA-I proteins is essential for functional HLA-I on the cell surface,' and B2M KO cells lose surface HLA-I. PMID 37627243 and 39057057 describe B2M as a constitutive component of Face-1 (canonical) MHC-I complexes on the cell surface. B2M has no TM domain of its own but is co-trafficked intracellularly as an obligate non-covalent partner of the MHC-I heavy chain (which is a classical single-pass TM protein), assembling in the ER and reaching the outer leaflet as a stable complex. This is the archetypal `stable_complex_partner` mechanism: assembled intracellularly and co-trafficked to the PM as a stable unit. Its surface presence is constitutive and broad, not restricted to a cell state or narrow lineage.
